algorithms.title: Asystole / PEA Cardiac Arrest Algorithm
algorithms.description: Step-by-step management of asystole and pulseless electrical activity (PEA), emphasizing nonshockable rhythm management, early epinephrine, and systematic identification and treatment of reversible causes (H's & T's).
steps: Identify asystole or PEA on the cardiac monitor — confirm on at least two ECG leads.
Immediately begin high-quality CPR: compressions at 100–120/min, depth 2–2.4 in, allow full recoil, with ventilations (bag-valve mask or advanced airway).
Establish IV or IO access as rapidly as possible.
Administer epinephrine 1 mg IV/IO (1:10,000) as soon as feasible — ideally within 3–5 minutes of arrest onset for nonshockable rhythms.
Continue CPR for 2 minutes, then perform a rhythm check.
If asystole or PEA persists: resume CPR, repeat epinephrine every 3–5 minutes, and systematically search for reversible causes (H's & T's).
If an organized rhythm appears: check for a pulse. If pulse present → ROSC pathway. If no pulse → continue as PEA.
If VF/pVT develops: immediately switch to the VF/pVT defibrillation algorithm.
Use waveform capnography (once advanced airway placed) to monitor CPR quality (target ETCO₂ ≥10 mmHg during compressions) and detect ROSC (sudden sustained rise).
Consider mechanical CPR only if manual compressions are impractical or unsafe (prolonged transport, limited personnel, ECPR cannulation).
Continue resuscitation with ongoing cause-directed treatment until ROSC or a decision to terminate is made.

algorithms.title: Systematic H's & T's Reversible Cause Evaluation
algorithms.description: A structured approach to identifying and treating the reversible causes of cardiac arrest during asystole/PEA resuscitation.
steps: H — Hypovolemia: assess for hemorrhage, dehydration. Treat with IV/IO fluid bolus and blood products if hemorrhagic.
H — Hypoxia: ensure adequate ventilation and oxygenation. Check airway patency, confirm advanced airway placement.
H — Hydrogen ion (acidosis): assess ABG. Treat with ventilation optimization; sodium bicarbonate for severe metabolic acidosis or specific indications.
H — Hypo-/Hyperkalemia: check potassium level. Treat hypokalemia with potassium replacement; treat hyperkalemia with calcium, insulin/glucose, sodium bicarbonate.
H — Hypothermia: check core temperature. Treat with warming measures if indicated.
T — Tension pneumothorax: assess breath sounds, tracheal deviation, JVD. Treat with immediate needle decompression → chest tube.
T — Tamponade (cardiac): assess for JVD, muffled heart sounds, pulsus paradoxus. Treat with emergent pericardiocentesis.
T — Toxins: consider drug overdose or poisoning. Treat with specific antidotes when available (e.g., sodium bicarbonate for TCA OD, naloxone for opioids).
T — Thrombosis (pulmonary): consider massive PE — sudden onset, right heart strain, risk factors. Treat with systemic thrombolysis or embolectomy.
T — Thrombosis (coronary): consider acute MI — ECG changes, chest pain history. Treat with emergent PCI when ROSC achieved.
